coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide by nanosecond pulsed laser ablation of graphene in liquid for bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Puliyasseri, Rohini
Haribabu, Jebiti
Ramirez-Tagle, Rodrigo
Sastikumar, Dillibabu
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) and nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (NGOs) by laser ablating graphene flakes in ethanol, utilizing liquid ammonia solution as the nitrogen source. Raman, FT-IR, and UV reveal the oxidation of graphene by laser ablation. Nitrogen doping was confirmed using FTIR and XPS analysis. XPS reveals the presence of pyrrolic, pyridinic, and graphitic nitrogen. The spherical shape and amorphous nature of NGOs are confirmed by HR-TEM analysis. FE-SEM examined the formation of different nanostructures of GO and NGO, and the average particle size was 40–50 nm. All samples show excitation-dependent PL emission with bluish-green emission, and nitrogen doping enhances the PL emission intensity. Time-resolved spectroscopy analysis shows the average lifetime improved by doping and increasing the nitrogen concentration. NGOs with remarkable quantum yields hold great promise for their application in bioimaging. We conducted cytotoxicity of the NGO samples against HeLa cancer cells using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay and selected the sample with lower cytotoxicity in cancer cells for bioimaging purposes. The NGOs with higher concentrations of nitrogen have less cytotoxicity and high photostability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) and nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (NGOs) by laser ablating graphene flakes in ethanol, utilizing liquid ammonia solution as the nitrogen source. Raman, FT-IR, and UV reveal the oxidation of graphene by laser ablation. Nitrogen doping was confirmed using FTIR and XPS analysis. XPS reveals the presence of pyrrolic, pyridinic, and graphitic nitrogen. The spherical shape and amorphous nature of NGOs are confirmed by HR-TEM analysis. FE-SEM examined the formation of different nanostructures of GO and NGO, and the average particle size was 40–50 nm. All samples show excitation-dependent PL emission with bluish-green emission, and nitrogen doping enhances the PL emission intensity. Time-resolved spectroscopy analysis shows the average lifetime improved by doping and increasing the nitrogen concentration. NGOs with remarkable quantum yields hold great promise for their application in bioimaging. We conducted cytotoxicity of the NGO samples against HeLa cancer cells using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay and selected the sample with lower cytotoxicity in cancer cells for bioimaging purposes. The NGOs with higher concentrations of nitrogen have less cytotoxicity and high photostability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a single layer of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice, has garnered significant attention in the research community due to its exceptional properties, including high Young's modulus, fracture strength, thermal conductivity, charge carrier mobility, and specific surface area [1–5]. However, the poor solubility of graphene in both polar and non-polar solvents has limited its applications. To overcome this challenge, graphene oxide (GO) emerged as a promising alternative, possessing hydrophilic properties due to the presence of oxygen functional groups on its surface [6,7].

Graphene-based nanomaterials, including graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and graphene nanosheets (GNs), have demonstrated tremendous potential in biomedical applications, such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and cancer therapy [8,9]. These nanomaterials exhibit a larger surface area and excellent solubility in various solvents, including water. The hydrogen bonding between the surface functional groups of GO and polar molecules contributes to its excellent dispersibility and hydrophilicity. However, its relatively poor conductivity hinders efficient charge transfer. The reduction of GO can partially restore the graphene structure, thereby improving conductivity [10]. Nevertheless, reduced GO tends to agglomerate and restack irreversibly due to van der Waals interactions, decreasing specific surface area and negatively impacting its electrochemical performance.

To address these limitations, researchers have explored the introduction of dopants or functional groups at the carbon basal planes and edge sites of graphene-based materials. Nitrogen has garnered significant attention among the various dopants due to its comparable atomic size and five valence electrons, which facilitate strong valence bonds with carbon atoms [11,12]. Nitrogen doping enhances graphene's electron-donor properties and binding ability, offering intriguing possibilities in high-frequency semiconductor devices, catalysis for energy conversion and storage, and improved biocompatibility for biosensing applications [13–16]. Moreover, NGO's exceptional surface activity enables the adsorption of drugs, biomolecules, and growth factors, making it suitable for in vitro cell imaging applications [17]. However, it is crucial to consider the potential cytotoxicity of NGOs, which depends on various physicochemical properties such as shape, size, oxidation methods, dispersion rate, dose administration, exposure time, and synthesis route.

Many existing synthesis methods are available to synthesize heteroatom-doped carbon nanoparticles, but they are complex, involving harsh chemicals, multiple purification steps, long reaction times, and high costs [17–20]. Preparation methods for nitrogen-doped graphene or its oxides include microwave irradiation [11], which needs a complex structure, and additional argon gas is required; the reflux method [21] needs strong acids and hydrothermal method is used nitrogen-doped samples, which will take hours of preparation, and purification steps. Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide was synthesized [22] by modified hummers method, and a good quality catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction was observed, again a complex reaction. Pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) offers precise control over the laser ablation process without the need for ultra-high vacuum conditions or the generation of toxic by-products [23–25]. This technique allows the synthesis of pure nanomaterials with reduced time consumption and the ability to tune the product by adjusting laser parameters [26–29].

Recent studies have utilized nanosecond-PLAL (ns-PLAL) to synthesize various carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanodiamonds, quantum dots like carbon dots, graphene quantum dots, and graphene oxide quantum dots, demonstrating the influence of solvent choice on their fluorescent properties [30–33]. There are different lasers available for the PLAL with a varity of wavelength [23]. The selection of a nanosecond pulsed Nd: YAG laser with a 1064 nm wavelength is a strategic choice to ensure efficient and controlled laser ablation of graphene, taking into account factors such as optimal absorption, non-thermal ablation, material compatibility, and the characteristics of Nd: Y3Al5O12(Nd: YAG) lasers [34,35].

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) were synthesized through PLAL [36,37] with high-purity graphite flakes(wavelength 355 nm and DETA nitrogen dopant) and carbon nano onion in liquid(532 nm and aqueous ammonia, pyridine and ethylenediamine as nitrogen dopants), and obtained material exhibited good photoluminescence and was suitable for optoelectronic and electrocatalytic application. The most suitable wavelength for PLAL of graphite and derivative is 1064 nm for Nd: YAG laser, and PLAL of Graphite target [32] gives single step preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene in liquid. All these PLAL methods are performed in target or by using liquid nitrogen as dopant. All these studies may result good fluroscent, but may be toxic and not suitable for cell studies.

One of the exciting areas of application for nanomaterials synthesized through PLAL is bioimaging. The field of bioimaging has witnessed remarkable advancements by the unique physicochemical properties of nanomaterials like graphene-based nanosheets. These nanomaterials, including graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and its hetero atom doped materials, exhibit exceptional properties such as large surface area, excellent solubility in various solvents, and surface functionalization capabilities. The presence of polar functional groups on the surface of GO enhances its dispersibility and hydrophilicity, making it an ideal candidate for use in bioimaging applications. Furthermore, the outstanding surface activity of GO allows for the adsorption of biomolecules, drugs, and growth factors, enabling its utilization in in-vitro cell imaging.

However, it's worth noting that the cytotoxicity of GO and similar nanomaterials can be a concern, and the outcome depends on several factors, including shape, size, synthesis method, and exposure conditions. However, the use of organic solvents like ethanol, acetone, and isopropyl alcohol has limited in vitro cytotoxicity analysis and bioimaging applications due to their disinfectant nature.

Our research group's article addresses this issue by presenting an approach that combines ns-PLAL with an additional post-ablation process to synthesize non-toxic and fluorescent graphene oxide-based nanomaterials in organic solvents [32,38,39]. The resulting materials, including graphene oxide, carbon dots, and graphene quantum dots, hold great potential for bioimaging applications. By exploring the synthesis of these materials using pulsed laser ablation in liquid, this study aims to overcome the limitations associated with graphene oxide, enhance their biocompatibility, and pave the way for their application in bioimaging.

In this study, a novel methodology was employed, involving the dispersion of graphene submicron flakes in ethanol instead of a target or pellet. This approach presents notable advantages over target ablation techniques due to its ability to cover a more extensive area in shorter timeframes. In contrast to target ablation, where only a limited surface area is exposed to laser irradiation, the utilization of an unfocused laser beam (with a diameter of 0.95 cm) directed into the beaker facilitates widespread ablation across a larger surface area within a brief time span. As a result, this innovative approach allows for the complete ablation of the prepared precursor solution and doping in a single step, showcasing remarkable efficiency. These combined characteristics position the Pulsed Laser Ablation in Liquids (PLAL) method as a highly recommended synthesis technique, demonstrating its capability to achieve thorough ablation and doping more efficiently than traditional target-based methods.

Moreover, drawing on previous endeavors within our research group, this investigation successfully accomplished heteroatom doping in a single step. The synthesized nano graphene oxides (NGOs) exhibited enhanced photoluminescence (PL) emission and prolonged lifetime, achievements attributed to the doping process. Notably, the sample's remarkable photostability is a crucial aspect in the context of potential applications. The introduction of nitrogen during doping renders the nanoparticles more functionally diverse and hydrophilic, making them well-suited for bioimaging applications. Thus, this research article presents a simple and efficient approach for synthesizing fluorescent and low-toxic, high-quantum yield nitrogen-doped graphene oxide via pulsed laser ablation in a liquid medium. By utilizing organic solvents and optimizing the post-ablation procedures, this research contributes to the development of safe and effective nanomaterials for bioimaging, potentially opening up new possibilities in the field of medical diagnostics and cellular imaging.

An Nd: YAG laser (Quanta ray, Spectra-Physics, Model no:230–10) with a fundamental laser wavelength of 1064 nm (unfocused laser beam with 0.95 cm diameter), a repetition rate of 10 Hz, and a pulse width of 10 ns was employed for the ablation. A detector (Coherent; model: FieldMaxII-TOP) was used to measure the energy emitted by the radiation. Sub-micron-sized Graphene powder (Alpha-easer, Ethanol spectrum) in ethanol (1 mg/mL) was ablated for 60 min with an unfocused beam of laser energy 625 mJ. Magnetic stirring ensured uniform distribution and avoided gravitational settling (Fig. 1 ). After 30 min of ablation, the solution was converted from black to pale yellow, confirming GO nanoparticle formation.

NGO was prepared separately by adding liquor ammonia solution (about 25 % NH3 from Fisher Scientific, 91 % pure) dropwise to the GO solution at the ablation time. After ablation, the pale-yellow color changed to brownish-yellow. Different concentrations of ammonia (3 mL(NGO1), 5 mL(NGO2), and 7 mL(NGO3)) were added to get different doping concentrations and ablated for 60 min. Fig. 2 shows the colloidal solution before and after ablation.

Post ablation technique was used to make the sample non-toxic. The solution dried at room temperature for three days. A brownish substance thus obtained is added with 100 mL double distilled water and sonicate for 30 min. The water-dispersed nanoparticles (NPs) are centrifuged with a microcentrifuge at 1500 rpm for 15 min to extract GO and NGO NPs. The collected GO and NGOs were dried at 70 °C for 12 h to remove the excess water content. The powdered NPs were sent for characterization without further modification. In Fig. 2, all samples exhibit the same concentration. Post laser ablation, the transition from black to pale yellow (Fig. 2(b)) signifies the conversion of graphene to graphene oxide (GO), marked by oxidation and the introduction of functional groups like carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxyl. Nitrogen doping (Fig. 2(c)) induces a color shift to brownish yellow, indicating the incorporation of nitrogen components—pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen—into the graphene lattice along with oxidation [40].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of synthesized colloidal nanoparticles were studied using a spectrometer (Model: JASCO V- 670) in the 200–700 nm range. The photoluminescent studies of GO and NGOs for different excitation wavelengths (320–420 nm) were carried out using a spectrofluorometer (Horbia: Fluromax). Lifetime analysis was done using TCSPC (Model: HORBIA Jobin Yvon). Structural and morphological analysis were carried out by HR-TEM and FE-SEM (CARL ZEISS (USA), Model: SIGMA WITH GEMINI COLUMN, Resolution 1.5 nm) with EDAX (BRUKER (GERMAN), Model: Nano XFlash Detector). Structural analysis was performed using XRD analysis (Model: PANalytical/ X Pert3) and Raman spectroscopy (WITec GmbH, Ulm, Germany, model: Alpha300RA AFM & RAMAN). Different elements present in the samples were analyzed using XPS analysis. Functional groups present in the compounds were found using FT-IR analysis (Model: Perkin Elmer- spectrum one).

The cytotoxicity of the prepared NGO samples was checked using the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay technique. HeLa, a cervical cancer cell, is used for the study. For maintaining the HeLa cells, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium was used, and the medium was seeded in 96 well plates, each containing 1 × 104 cells. These cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. To plant the cells in the wells, they are dissolved in DMSO. After incubation, a dye solution of MTT (10 μL,5 mg/mL) was poured into each well and maintained at the same temperature for 4 h. Then the compound's absorbance value was quantified at (405 nm). For each NGO sample, the IC50 value, which represents the concentration of the same substance that causes a 50 % deduction, was estimated. [41,42].

Graphene oxide and nitrogen–doped graphene oxide was synthesized by nanosecond pulsed laser ablation of graphene in ethanol. Liquor ammonia is used as the dopant. GO and three samples with different nitrogen concentrations (NGO1, NGO2, and NGO3) were made by 1064 nm laser irradiation for 1 h with 625 mJ energy.

UV–vis absorbance spectra were obtained to characterize the optical absorbance of the colloidal solutions and confirm the nature of the carbon nanostructures. Fig. 3 (a-d) shows the UV absorbance spectra of GO and NGOs. In the GO absorbance spectrum, the peak around 230 is due to π–π* transition of the CC bond in graphitic carbon. A shoulder peak around 302 nm due to n–π* transitions of CO bonds of oxidized carbon matches previous reports [38]. In the NGO1 spectrum, the peak at 230 arises due to π–π* transition of graphitic sp2 domains [43]. The peak at 254 nm corresponds to the graphitic carbon's n–π* transition, and the peak at 302 nm indicates the n–π* transition of CO. In NGO2 and NGO3, the absorbance peaks corresponding to π–π* transition of graphitic carbon(C=C), n–π* of CC, and n–π* transition of CO can be observed in each absorption spectrum. Fig. 3(b-d) represents the absorbance spectra of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide samples. The only difference is in the position of π–π* transition of graphitic carbon, and there is a red shift in the absorbance wavelength. It shifted from 230 nm to 239 nm as the nitrogen doping concentration increased. The redshift of the peaks (arising due to π–π* transition) in the graphitic carbon region indicates the reduction of GO or removal of the oxygen group due to increased nitrogen concentration [23]. No sample exhibits absorption peaks longer than 400 nm, showing that all absorbance shifts occur in the UV blue area. From this, it can be assumed that GO and NGOs have minimal interaction with the 1064 nm laser during PLAL [37].

Fig. 4 (a-d) displays the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the synthesized GO and NGOs. The excitation wavelength varies from 320 to 420 nm, and all the samples show excitation-dependent PL emission [38]. The origin of the excitation-dependent PL emission can be traced back to the radiative recombination of the electron-hole pair, which arises from a localized small sp2 hybridized carbon cluster embedded within the sp3 matrix. The electrons make a transition from HOMO level to LUMO level in each cluster of sp3 hybridized carbon. Fig. 5 shows the schematic diagram of PL emission.

When exposed to laser irradiation, the sp2 domains in GO contract and disperse within the oxygenated sp3 hybridized carbon matrix. In GO samples, the maximum intensity emission wavelength is 423 nm, with the excitation at 380 nm. The emission intensity varies from 396 to 480 nm with an increase in excitation wavelength. NGO samples with different nitrogen concentrations also exhibit similar behavior. In NGOs, emission intensity varies from 411 to 482 nm. For all the samples, the PL intensity peak shifts monotonically towards the higher wavelength with rapid change in intensity [35]. In GO and NGO samples, emission peak intensity initially increased and then decreased when excited from 320 to 420 nm. It may be due to the presence of sp2 hybridized nanodomain in the samples. The large nanodomain also led to the redshift in the peak intensity [44]. All the samples have bright bluish-green emissions around 475 nm when excited with 380 nm. Similar properties of GOQs have also been reported [23]. The absence of the CC bond's n-π* transition in NGO2 doesn't affect its photoluminescence spectra. Other recombination pathways, possibly influenced by electronic or structural properties, compensate for this absence, minimizing its impact on the overall spectra [45].

Fig. 6(a) shows the plot of the maximum intensity emission of GO and NGOs. An enhanced PL emission with a blue shift in intensity position is observed due to increased doping concentration [44]. It can be observed that the maximum intensity is for 380 nm excitation for all the samples, and the position of maximum intensity shifts from 468 to 463 nm. The introduction of nitrogen doping enhances the PL emission of GO, resulting in a blue shift in the PL spectrum. Nitrogen is considered an electron acceptor in this context. XPS of NGOs N1s spectra can prove these results; the higher pyrrolic nitrogen favors the red shift in PL spectra, and the pyridinic nitrogen component gives the blue shift in PL emission, which will be discussed in detail in the XPS analysis section. The enhanced PL intensity suggests that NGO3 is most suitable for bioimaging, even though cytotoxicity should also be considered.

The origin of the optical properties of CNPs is still an exciting area of the research community. Time-resolved spectroscopy is carried out to investigate the recombination mechanism further. The fluorescence decay measurement was conducted at 430 nm using an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The intensity decay profile tracks the kinetics of electron deactivation from an excited state to the ground state through both radiative and nonradiative pathways [46]. Fig. 6(b) shows the Fluorescence lifetime curve for NGO1, NGO2, and NGO3 with the correction factor IRF. Time-resolved spectroscopic studies are performed to investigate the recombination mechanism of the GO and NGOs. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of NGO1 and NGO2 are fitted with the bi-exponential function, and GO and NGO3 are fitted using the triexponential function. The normalized fitting parameters are given in Table 1 . The average lifetime can be calculated by the equation,

Each decay exhibited a rapid component arising from the radiative recombination of intrinsic states, along with additional delayed components attributed to the presence of surface functional groups in the sample [47]. Here, in all samples, τ1 is the fast component, τ2 is the delayed component in two exponential fits (NGO1 and NGO2), and τ2 and τ3 are the two delayed components in three exponential fits (for GO and NGO3). The average lifetime of GO is improved by laser ablation synthetic root and by increasing ablation time [23,48]. After doping nitrogen, the fastest component (τ1) is the least, and the delayed components, including the functional group component, have the major contribution [37].

The average lifetime of GO is 2.73 ns, and the decay curve is shown in supporting information (Fig. S3). NGO3 offers a higher lifetime value of 5.83 ns; for NGO1 and NGO2, the average lifetime is 2.59 ns and 3.39 ns, respectively. The lifetime obtained for all these NGOs is greater than other reported values of NGOs by laser irradiation method, and the average lifetime is improved by laser ablation and nitrogen doping [24,49]. It may be due to increased fluorophore density and decreased nonradiative traps. The improved lifetime with enhanced PL emission suggests that NGOs will be promising candidates for bioimaging.

A relative measurement method was used to determine the quantum yield of the obtained NGOs. Rhodamine B, with a known quantum yield (QR = 0.31 in water), was chosen as the reference dye at 380 nm in a 1 cm quartz cuvette. To reduce self-absorption effects, all samples were appropriately diluted to keep their optical densities below 0.1. Integrated PL intensity of NGOs and Rhodamine B was plotted at various concentrations. The quantum yield was then calculated using the provided Eq. (2),
(2)
Q S
=
Q R
Grad
S
Grad
R
η S
η R
where “R” and “S” represent the reference and sample, respectively, QS and QR denote the quantum yield of the sample and reference. Grad stands for the gradient of the plot illustrating the integral area of PL intensity against absorbance. Additionally, η represents the refractive index of the solvent used, with a value of 1.33 for water and 1.36 for anhydrous ethanol [50].

The Quantum Yield of GO is 0.013; for NGO3, it is found to be 0.164; for NGO2, the quantum yield is 0.113; and for NGO1, the quantum yield is found to be 0.08. The quantum yield of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide increases by doping(1.3 % to 16.4 %). All these values are much better than reported quantum yield values by PLAL and nitrogen doping in carbon materials [49]. The remarkable quantum yield achieved for NGOs holds great promise for their application in bioimaging, affirming their excellence as a material of choice.

Fig. 7(a) displays the FT-IR spectra of GO and NGOs within the range of 500–4000 cm−1. It is the first confirmation method to check the Nitrogen doping in GO. The characteristic bands caused by the graphene oxide functional groups were visible in the FT-IR spectrum, and these bands are consistent with earlier reports for graphene oxide produced chemically [11,51]. Most of the peaks in GO are present in NGOs, with increased intensities. GO shows the characteristic peaks at 3581 cm−1, 851 cm−1, 1268 cm−1, and 773 cm−1, representing the OH stretching vibration, CC bending vibration, CO stretching vibration, and CC bending vibration, respectively. The peaks observed at 2973 cm−1 to 3078 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 to 3012 cm−1 represent the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of the CH bond, respectively. Peaks in the region 1384 cm−1 to 1524 cm−1 are for the CH /O–H bending functional groups in the GO and NGO samples.

In NGOs, all these peaks are visible with increased intensity. The peaks observed in the 1100 cm−1 to 1103 cm−1 range indicate the CN stretching vibration, and peaks observed in the region 1626 cm−1 to 1630 cm−1 indicate NH bending vibration, which confirms the nitrogen doping in the sample. The broadened OH stretching region shifts to the lower wavenumber region, indicating the stretching vibration of the NH bond, which is visible in all nitrogen-doped samples [37]. The peak at 1735 cm−1 represents vibrational frequency of COOH, which maybe due to the water content from liquor ammonia in NGOs and it is absent for GO. The relative intensities of nitrogen functional groups are increased with the increase in doping concentration [21,37]. The above results show many hydroxy groups, indicating the samples' hydrophilic properties. All these functional groups confirm the formation of GO and NGOs with different nitrogen concentrations [24,38,51,52].

Raman spectroscopy offers a non-destructive approach to investigate the structural properties of graphene-based materials [53]. The Raman spectrometer used for analysis has 600 lines/mm grating, and the resolution is 5 cm−1. Fig. 7(b and c) shows the Raman spectra of the samples before and after laser ablation. Fig. 6 (b) shows the Raman spectra of precursor graphene and GO, and Fig. 7(c) shows the Raman spectra of NGO samples with different nitrogen concentrations. The spectra consist of two divergent bands at 1339 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1 for the precursor graphene and 1358 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1 of GO, corresponding to the characteristic band of graphitic structure named D band and G band [35]. The D band can indicate sp3 defects in the graphitic carbon or represent a breakdown in the translational symmetry within the perfect graphitic structure. The G band originates from vibrations in the sp2 hybridized carbon, resulting from the first-order scattering of the E2g phonon at the Brillouin zone boundary [54]. After ablation, the G band exhibits broadening and shifts to a higher wavenumber, which is attributed to the formation of new sp3 carbon atoms in the graphene lattice [55]. Graphene oxide exhibits a band at 2751 cm−1 in its Raman spectra, referred to as a 2D band and is a Raman signature of sp2 graphitic materials in addition to the D and G bands. The 2D band is an overtone of D and is associated with the stacking order of the GO lattice. The 2D band shifts to lower frequencies and diminishes intensity when compared to graphene; similar results have been reported by researchers [23]. The blue shift in the 2D band of GO indicates a reduction in the material, resulting from enhanced coupling and the elimination of trapped interlayer species and functional groups. Raman spectral bands like D″ and D + G, which are visible in the GO at 1008 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1, respectively, result from the breathing-like modes of carbon rings that are activated by the double resonance Raman process [38].

The Raman spectra of NGO samples are shown in Fig. 7(c). All the characteristic peaks like D, G, and the 2D band are clearly visible in the Raman spectra of NGOs, and the band positions are given in Table 2 . From the Raman spectra, the intensity of bands decreased by doping and increasing the concentration of nitrogen in the sample. By doping, the peak position of the G band shifted to the lower frequencies compared with the graphene oxide [12]. The shift in D and G bands is because of the structural distortion of graphene oxide. It may be due to the difference in the bond lengths of CC and CN. It is already reported that electron-donating species cause a downshift in the G band [12,21].

In GO and NGOs, the G band intensity is reduced by laser ablation and increases in doping concentration. From Fig. 7(c), the 2D band intensity diminishes abruptly by comparing Fig. 7(b), and then the I2D/IG ratio, which represents the number of layers, decreases (I2D/IG < 1)and which confirms all samples are multilayer in nature [22].

The Tunistra-Koenig relation can be used to determine the average crystallite size of sp2 carbon domains.

The ratio of ID/IG increased after ablation. The size of the sp2 domain decreases about 1.3 times from 40.64 nm (of graphene) to 30.00 nm (for GO). It is reduced to 21.59 nm (∼2 times) for NGOs, suggesting that PLAL can cause the nucleation of the sp2 domain within the sp3 matrix of graphene. Increase in

ratio shows more sp3 hybridized carbon in GO and NGOs compared to precursor graphene. This change in

ratio suggests that the sp2 domain size is decreased by graphene's exfoliation, which confirms the formation of GO and NGOs. A decrease in different peak intensities and increased ID/IG values is the measure of a reduction in the crystallinity of the samples, concluding that the NGOs are highly amorphous [38]. The SAED pattern from HR-TEM(Fig. S2) supports the amorphous nature. The defect density of the samples can be calculated by Eq. (4), which shows that the defect is increased by laser ablation and doping with nitrogen. From Table 3 , the sample with the largest La value has a minor density defect.
The X-ray diffraction pattern of precursor Graphene and GO is given in supporting information (Fig. S1), and the amorphous nature of NGOs is shown in the HR-TEM image with the SAED pattern (Fig. S2).

Fig. 10(a) shows the XPS spectrum of the NGO2 sample and its deconvoluted peaks. In the survey spectrum, the peak at ∼400 eV confirms the presence of nitrogen in the sample. The constituent elements' peaks are deconvoluted. The C1s spectra can be fitted into three components, as shown in Fig. 9(b); the peaks correspond to CC for sp2 hybridized carbon, CO for the hydroxyl group, and CN group. The N1s spectra shown in Fig. 10(c) is deconvoluted into three segments at 398.55 eV, 399.65 eV, and 400.92 eV. Each of these peaks represents pyridinic, pyrrolic, and Graphitic Nitrogen contents, respectively. The three most frequent bonding arrangements that a nitrogen atom has when it is doped into graphene and its derivative materials are quaternary (also known as graphitic nitrogen), pyridinic, and pyrrolic nitrogen. Pyridinic nitrogen specifically forms a bond with two C atoms at the edges or defects of graphene and adds one p electron to the system. Pyrrolic nitrogen refers to nitrogen atoms that give the system two p electrons, though they do not always bond into the five-membered ring as in pyrrole. Quaternary nitrogen refers to nitrogen atoms that replace C atoms in the hexagonal ring. Similar observations were made by Madhukumar et al. The amount of pyridinic nitrogen increases with doping, and the other two components decrease accordingly. The increased pyridinic nitrogen is the reason for the enhanced PL emission of the NGOs.

The third distinct peak shown in Fig. 10(d), singlet O1s, also deconvoluted into three components at a binding energy of 531.63 eV (C=O), 532.13 eV (CO), and 532.46 eV (O–C=O) which corresponds to the oxygenious functional group attached within or on the surface of the sample.

Supporting information include the XPS spectra and its deconvoluted peak details (Table. S1) of GO (Fig. S4), NGO1 (Fig. S5), and NGO3 (Fig. S6). In the C1s spectrum of GO (Fig. S4. (b)), the peak at 282.88 eV corresponds to sp2 aromatic carbon, while the sharp peak at 284.36 eV represents the sp3 CO bond. Additionally, two peaks at 286.36 and 287.88 eV indicate the presence of CO and C-O-C/OH bonding, respectively [51].

Doping GO with nitrogen brings about significant changes in the C1s peak observed in its XPS spectrum, primarily due to the introduction of nitrogen-containing functional groups. In the C1s spectra of NGO1 (Fig. S5(b)), features such as CC corresponding to sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon appear at 283.98 and 285.03 eV, respectively. A decrease in the intensity of the sp2 CC peak suggests oxidation, and the introduction of nitrogen results in the presence of the C-NH2 bond at 285.73 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen [37]. In the C1s spectra of NGO2 (Fig. 10(b)) and NGO3 (Fig. S6(b)), the presence of the CN bond is evident at binding energies around ∼285–286 eV. NGO2 exhibits additional functional groups such as CC (283.56 eV) and CO (285.95 eV). NGO3 samples also contain oxygen-containing or carboxylic functional groups like CO and C-O-C at 286.36 and 286.39 eV, respectively [21,56].

Across all nitrogen-doped samples, the C1s spectra encompass nitrogen functional groups in the energy range of 258–286 eV, attributed to the incorporation of Pyridine-type nitrogen, which enhance the fluorescence [21,56]. The overall broadening of C1s spectra in the doped samples, compared to GO, facilitates the deconvolution of peaks into individual components corresponding to different carbon‑nitrogen bonding configurations. In NGOs the intensities of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups is higher than that of GO indicates more oxygen and nitrogen incorporated functional groups are included in NGOs contributing to their hydrophilicity [23,51].

The FE-SEM images of GO and NGO1(Fig. 11 (a & d)) possess agglomerated spherical structures with an average size of 180 nm and 150 nm, respectively. In the case of NGO2, the nano coral structure can be seen with a porous nature. In NGO3(Fig. 11(g)), agglomerated spherical with sheet-like morphology can be seen in higher magnification. In NGO2 and NGO3, the average size is 46.86 and 49.22 nm, respectively (Fig. 11 (f&i)). As already reported by Yogesh et al., the laser-ablated synthesized GOs are fused to form bigger particles. Similar behavior is noticed in the prepared GOs and NGOs [35]. Even though the particles are agglomerated, small nanoparticles can be seen in the images.

From the EDAX analysis of NGOs, the atomic percentage of different elements of NGO1 (Fig. 11(b)) is carbon- 75.63 %, oxygen-18.07 %, and nitrogen- 5.19%. For the NGO2 sample (Fig. 11(e)), the atomic percentage of carbon is 78.23 %, nitrogen is 6.12 %, and oxygen is 15.65 %. Similarly, in the case of NGO3 (Fig. 11(h)), carbon-75.46 %, oxygen-17.69 %, and nitrogen - 6.86 %, respectively, and the EDAX results are almost comparable with the XPS analysis.

The supporting information includes an illustrative diagram displaying a magnified section of FE-SEM image depicting GO structures (Fig. S7). The image clearly exhibits the merging of smaller-sized GOs. In the figure, two GO nanospheres are shown as they come into contact with each other. Furthermore, the partial and complete fusion of two GO nanospheres demonstrates the gradual growth of the GO nanospheres as they engulf neighboring GO nanospheres. The release of oxygen-containing components during a 12-h heat treatment at 70 °C may be the cause of the surface roughness seen on the GO nanospheres. Similar behavior exists for NGOs, which is the reason for the increased size of NGO1 and GO samples. [35,37]

The HR-TEM images shown in the supplementary information(Fig. S2) confirm the samples' spherical nature and the average particle size is only 10–12 nm. The agglomerate structure in FE-SEM may be due to the time delay. Increased particle size in FE-SEM may be due to the fusing of nanoparticles in the case of GO and NGO1. The XRD patterns of Graphene and GO is shown in supporting information. The high value of interplanar spacing from XRD (Fig. S1)analysis of GO may be due to the intercalation of the -OH group between the layers of GO, and all results agrees with the reported literature [38].

The formation of GO and NGOs is confirmed from the structural, optical, and morphological studies. Based on their high quantum yield, fluorescent, and lifetime properties, NGOs are a promising candidate for Bioimaging studies. The presence of polar functional groups on the surface of NGO enhances its dispersibility and hydrophilicity, making it an ideal candidate for use in bioimaging applications, even though cytotoxicity studies should be performed to fix the material's biocompatibility.

HeLa cells are derived from cervical cancer cells and are used in research because they are widely available, have uniform characteristics, and can be used in various lab environments. They have been used for decades as a standard for cross-disciplinary study validation because they provide a flexible and reliable model for evaluating cellular responses to treatments. The next section of this work performs the cell studies, followed by Imaging the less cytotoxic sample in HeLa cancer cells.

Based on optical properties, the prepared NGOs are suitable for bioimaging applications. For investigating biocompatibility, cytotoxicity studies were carried out in HeLa cancer cells. The Fig. 12 shows the percentage cell viability for different sample concentrations, and the IC50 value is found and is given in Table 5 . The results show that NGO1 has less cell viability than other samples. NGO3 is low cytotoxicity and is used for bioimaging applications. The less toxicity of the NGO may be attributed to its hydrophilicity and improved dispersity, which result from the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups attached to the surface. Even though NGOs have functional groups on their surface, a delocalized π electron in their basal plane exhibits the covalent bonding that allows drugs and active substances to bind to them, and this can be used for in-vitro bioimaging.

The MTT assay was illuminated with 405 nm, and after an incubation of 1 h, the microscopy images were recorded. The synthesized NGOs are well penetrated efficiently into the HeLa cancer cell and diffused uniformly all over the HeLa cancer cell cytoplasmic region with bright blue emission (Fig. 13 ). In overly image, all other cell components are visible as white patches, without any emission. These results show that the synthesized NGOs could be profitably used as biomarkers or bioimaging probes.

Graphene oxide and nitrogen-doped graphene oxide were successfully synthesized by a simple laser ablation technique. The surface morphology is determined using FE-SEM. The shape of the synthesized GO is an agglomerated spherical structure, and NGOs exhibit nano coral and spherical structure with 40–50 nm in size. HR-TEM reveals NGOs' amorphous nature and spherical shape. Raman studies reveal the multilayer nature of the samples with decreased crystallinity and average crystalline size of sp2 domains with doping. FT-IR analysis confirms the formation of GO and NGOs, and it consists of hydroxy groups, which indicates the sample is hydrophilic. XPS analysis shows the nitrogen doping in the sample, and the pyridinic pyrrolic and graphitic behavior exists in all NGO samples. Lifetime analysis of the excitation-dependent PL emission shows the synthesized NGOs have good fluorescence with blueish-green emission and a higher lifetime, which can be suitable for bioimaging applications. Doping of nitrogen enhanced the lifetime and PL emission properties. The remarkable quantum yield achieved in PLAL-synthesized NGOs holds great promise for their bioimaging application. In-vitro studies demonstrated that the synthesized NGO is less cytotoxic towards HeLa cancer cells, and almost all cells survived for the NGO3 sample for higher concentration. Within one hour, the NGO3 nanoparticles were observed to be internalized by cells without causing any impact on their PL emission, suggesting that the NGO3 nanoparticles possess excellent photostability in the biological environment. Consequently, these findings demonstrate that the laser ablation technique is a straightforward and dependable approach for synthesizing fluorescent and non-toxic NGO nanoparticles. These nanoparticles hold great potential for future exploration and utilization in various biomedical applications.
